target.name: i-A2E
target.motivation: The total synthesis of iiso-A2E was undertaken to confirm its proposed structure, which is an ocular pyridinium bisretinoid found in retinal pigment epithelium lipofuscin. The synthesis ultimately led to a stereochemical reassignment of the natural product to i-A2E.

success_criteria.free_text: The primary success criterion was to synthesize the proposed structure of iiso-A2E and compare its spectroscopic data with the natural product. If discrepancies arose, the goal was to synthesize alternative stereoisomers to identify the correct structure of the natural product.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond forming the pentaenyl chain at C3 of the pyridine ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: The HWE reaction is a classical method for constructing polyenes with E-selectivity, which was initially envisaged for both polyenic substituents.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond forming the tetraenyl chain at C4 of the pyridine ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: Pd-catalyzed Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling was chosen to construct the C10-C11 bond, offering an alternative to HWE for potentially better stereoselectivity.

key_disconnections.bond_description: N-C bond forming the pyridinium ion
key_disconnections.reasoning: Alkylation of the pyridine nitrogen with 2-iodoethanol is a standard method to form pyridinium salts.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The retrosynthetic strategy involved a convergent approach, building the two polyene chains separately and then attaching them to a pyridine core. The initial plan relied heavily on Horner-Wadsworth-Emmons (HWE) condensations for polyene construction. However, due to stereoselectivity issues with HWE for one of the chains, a Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling strategy was adopted for that specific bond formation. The final step involved pyridine alkylation to form the pyridinium salt. This stepwise approach allowed for the synthesis of different stereoisomers to resolve the structural ambiguity of the natural product.

thought.background: The structure of iiso-A2E (8) was previously assigned based on NMR and NOESY data, suggesting a Z geometry at the C13'=C14' olefin.
thought.gap: The authors noted that the C13' chemical shift in the 13C NMR spectrum of natural iiso-A2E (8) was inconsistent with a Z geometry, which typically appears around 20 ppm, whereas the reported value was 12.80 ppm. This suggested a potential misassignment of the double bond geometry.
thought.decision: To confirm the proposed structure of iiso-A2E (8) and resolve the discrepancy, a total synthesis was initiated. The initial synthetic plan aimed to construct the proposed iiso-A2E (8) and then compare it to the natural product.

outcome.observation: Identified a potential stereochemical misassignment in the reported structure of iiso-A2E (8).

references: Li, J.; Yao, K.; Yu, X.; Dong, X.; Gan, L.; Luo, C.; Wu, Y. Identification of a Novel Lipofuscin Pigment (iisoA2E) in Retina and Its Effects in the Retinal Pigment Epithelial Cells. J. Biol. Chem. 2013, 288, 35671−35682.

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, whereas the E geometry was assigned by 1H NMR spectroscopic analysis to most of the di- and trisubstituted double bonds of iiso-A2E (8 (Figure 1B)) using the value of coupling constants and/or NOE correlations, the Z geometry attributed to the trisubstituted C13′=C14′ olefin was proposed by apparent NOESY correlations observed between C13′-CH3 and C14′–H.14 The structural proposal for iiso-A2E (8 (Figure 1B))14 as a positional isomer of iso-A2E (7) caught our attention. As a follow-up to our contributions to the stereocontrolled synthesis of bioactive polyenes,17−20 we addressed its total synthesis with the aim to confirm the proposed structure of this structurally unusual pyridinium bisretinoid.14

thought.background: The synthesis of (Z)-alkenylstannane 15 was required as a component for the synthesis of dialdehyde 12, which was an intermediate in the initial retrosynthetic plan for iiso-A2E (8).
thought.gap: Need to synthesize (Z)-alkenylstannane 15 from propargylic alcohol 13.
thought.decision: To synthesize 15, the previously described method involving stereoselective addition of lithium dimethylcuprate to 13, trapping with I2, and protection to afford alkenyl iodide 14, followed by treatment with t-BuLi and n-Bu3SnCl, was chosen.

outcome.observation: (Z)-alkenylstannane 15 was obtained in 39% combined yield.

references: Bulger, P. G.; Moloney, M. G.; Trippier, P. C. A multicomponent coupling strategy suitable for the synthesis of the triene component of the oxazolomycin antibiotics. Org. Biomol. Chem. 2003, 1, 3726−3737.

evidence.text_span: As a component for the synthesis of dialdehyde 12, (Z)- alkenylstannane 1528,29 was obtained from propargylic alcohol 13 as previously described28 in a 39% combined yield. Stereoselective addition of lithium dimethylcuprate to 13, trapping with I2,30−32 and protection afforded alkenyl iodide 14,28,33 which was treated with t-BuLi34 and then with n-Bu3SnCl at −78 °C to provide 15.

thought.background: (Z)-alkenylstannane 15 was synthesized. The next step was to couple it with 3-bromoisonicotinaldehyde 16 to form (Z)-2-alkenyl-substituted isonicotinaldehyde 17.
thought.gap: Need to perform a cross-coupling reaction between 15 and 16.
thought.decision: The Stille-Migita-Kosugi cross-coupling reaction was chosen, using Pd(PPh3)2Cl2 and PPh3 in toluene at 110 °C.

outcome.observation: (Z)-2-alkenyl-substituted isonicotinaldehyde 17 was obtained in 90% yield.

references: Farina, V.; Roth, G. P. Recent Advances in the Stille Reaction; JAI Press: Greenwich, CT, 1996; Vol. 5, pp 1−53.

evidence.text_span: Stille−Migita−Kosugi cross-coupling reaction35−38 with commercial 3-bromoisonicotinaldehyde 16 promoted by Pd(PPh3)2Cl2 and PPh3 in toluene15 at 110 °C (Scheme 2) afforded (Z)-2-alkenyl-substituted isonicotinaldehyde 17 in 90% yield.

thought.background: Intermediate 17 was successfully synthesized. The original plan was to deprotect 17 and then perform allylic oxidation to form dialdehyde 12.
thought.gap: Need to deprotect the silyl ether in 17.
thought.decision: Attempts were made to deprotect 17 using TBAF or acidic media.

outcome.observation: Deprotection of 17 with TBAF or acidic media led to undesired secondary products.

evidence.text_span: Unfortunately, attempts to deprotect 17 with TBAF or acidic media prior to allylic oxidation on route to dialdehyde 12 led to undesired secondary products, and therefore, we selected instead the stepwise construction of the polyene fragments at positions C4 and C3 of the pyridine skeleton present in 8.

thought.background: The direct deprotection and oxidation of 17 to dialdehyde 12 failed due to undesired side products.
thought.gap: A new strategy is needed to construct the polyene fragments on the pyridine core.
thought.decision: The strategy was revised to a stepwise construction of the polyene fragments at positions C4 and C3 of the pyridine skeleton, rather than forming a dialdehyde intermediate.

outcome.observation: Revised strategy to stepwise construction of polyene fragments.

evidence.text_span: Unfortunately, attempts to deprotect 17 with TBAF or acidic media prior to allylic oxidation on route to dialdehyde 12 led to undesired secondary products, and therefore, we selected instead the stepwise construction of the polyene fragments at positions C4 and C3 of the pyridine skeleton present in 8.

thought.background: Following the revised strategy, the first polyene chain (C4-tetraenyl) needed to be constructed. The HWE condensation of 17 with heptatrienylphosphonate 11 was considered.
thought.gap: Need to perform the HWE condensation to form the C4-tetraenyl-substituted pyridine.
thought.decision: The HWE condensation of 17 with an excess of the anion generated from 11 (1.7 molar equivalents) using NaHMDS (1.5 molar equivalents) in THF and DMPU was chosen.

outcome.observation: The C4-tetraenyl-substituted pyridine was formed as a ca. 2:1 mixture of E and Z isomers (18 and 19, respectively) in 86% yield. However, the stereoselectivity was low.

evidence.text_span: The best reaction conditions for the HWE condensation reaction21−26 of 17 with heptatrienylphosphonate 1118,27 used an excess (1.7 molar equivalents) of the anion generated with NaHMDS (1.5 molar equivalents) in THF and DMPU and afforded the C4-tetraenyl-substituted pyridine as a ca. 2:1 mixture of E and Z isomers (18 and 19, respectively) in 86% yield (Scheme 2).39

thought.background: The HWE condensation for the C4-tetraenyl chain resulted in low stereoselectivity (2:1 E/Z mixture).
thought.gap: A more stereoselective method is needed for the C4-tetraenyl chain construction.
thought.decision: Switched to a Pd-catalyzed Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling reaction for the construction of the C10-C11 bond, starting from 17.

outcome.observation: Adopted Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling to improve stereoselectivity for the C4-tetraenyl chain.

evidence.text_span: As an alternative to overcome the low stereoselectivity of the HWE condensation, we turned our attention to the construction of the C10−C11 bond using Pd-catalyzed Suzuki−Miyaura cross-coupling reactions.40−42

thought.background: To use the Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling, alkenylboronic acid 21 was needed. This could be formed from 17.
thought.gap: Need to convert 17 into alkenylboronic acid 21.
thought.decision: Treatment of 17 with bis[(pinacolato)boryl]methane 20 and lithium 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (boron-Wittig olefination variant) was chosen.

outcome.observation: Alkenylboronic acid 21 was obtained in 62% yield, along with minor amounts of the expected alkenylpinacolboronate.

evidence.text_span: Alkenyl extension at position C4 of the pyridine ring with functionalization as alkenylboronate was carried upon treatment of 17 with bis[(pinacolato)boryl]methane 20 and lithium 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine using the boron-Wittig olefination variant.43 The reaction afforded alkenylboronic acid 21 in 62% yield and minor amounts of the expected alkenylpinacolboronate, as confirmed by NMR characterization after flash-column chromatography.

thought.background: Alkenylboronic acid 21 was synthesized. Now, it needed to be coupled with trienyl iodide 22 to form the C3-alkenyl,C4-tetraenylpyridine 18.
thought.gap: Need to perform the Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling reaction.
thought.decision: The Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling of 21 and 22 was performed in THF/H2O with catalytic amounts of Pd(PPh3)4 and Tl2CO3.

outcome.observation: Targeted C3-alkenyl,C4-tetraenylpyridine 18 was formed in 74% yield.

evidence.text_span: Upon stirring solutions of 21 and trienyl iodide 2244 in THF/H2O mixtures with catalytic amounts of Pd(PPh3)4 and promoted by Tl2CO3, targeted C3-alkenyl,C4-tetraenylpyridine 18 was formed in 74% yield.

thought.background: Intermediate 18 was obtained. The silyl protecting group needed to be removed to allow for further functionalization.
thought.gap: Need to deprotect 18 to form 23.
thought.decision: Deprotection of 18 was carried out using HCO2H in H2O at 0 °C for 15 hours.

outcome.observation: Deprotection of 18 yielded 23 in 52% yield.

evidence.text_span: Deprotection of 18 (HCO2H, H2O, 0 °C, 15 h, 52%) was followed by allylic alcohol oxidation of 23 using MnO2 with Na2CO3 to afford Z-enal 24 in 85% yield.

thought.background: Allylic alcohol 23 was obtained. The next step was to oxidize it to the corresponding enal 24.
thought.gap: Need to perform allylic alcohol oxidation of 23.
thought.decision: Allylic alcohol oxidation of 23 was performed using MnO2 with Na2CO3.

outcome.observation: Z-enal 24 was afforded in 85% yield.

evidence.text_span: Deprotection of 18 (HCO2H, H2O, 0 °C, 15 h, 52%) was followed by allylic alcohol oxidation of 23 using MnO2 with Na2CO3 to afford Z-enal 24 in 85% yield.

thought.background: Z-enal 24 was obtained. The second HWE reaction was planned to construct the pentaene branch using phosphonate 11.
thought.gap: Need to perform the HWE condensation of 24 with 11.
thought.decision: The HWE reaction of 24 with excess 11 was performed under the same conditions as previously described (NaHMDS, THF, DMPU).

outcome.observation: Bis-polyenyl pyridine 10 was afforded in 60% yield. This reaction was highly stereoselective for the construction of the pentaene branch.

evidence.text_span: The second HWE reaction21−26 of 24 with excess 11 under the same conditions described above was highly stereoselective for the construction of the pentaene branch and afforded 10 in 60% yield.

thought.background: Bis-polyenyl pyridine 10 was synthesized, representing the proposed structure of iiso-A2E (8) before alkylation. The final step for the proposed iiso-A2E (8) was pyridine alkylation.
thought.gap: Need to alkylate pyridine 10 with 2-iodoethanol (25).
thought.decision: Pyridine alkylation of 10 with 2-iodoethanol (25) was performed in nitromethane upon heating to 60 °C for 18 hours, to prevent isomerization.

outcome.observation: iiso-A2E (8) was afforded in 45% yield.

evidence.text_span: Careful pyridine alkylation of 10 with 2-iodoethanol (25) in nitromethane upon heating to 60 °C for 18 h (to prevent the isomerization occurring when using refluxing conditions at 100 °C as reported by Nakanishi for the synthesis of A2E (6)15) afforded iiso-A2E (8) in 45% yield.

thought.background: Synthetic iiso-A2E (8) was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to compare the spectroscopic data of synthetic 8 with the reported data for natural iiso-A2E (8).
thought.decision: 1H NMR and UV spectroscopic data were compared.

outcome.observation: Subtle differences were noted in the 1H NMR spectra, particularly in the pyridinium ion region and methyl groups. The UV absorption maxima of synthetic 8 (λmax ≈ 340 and 430 nm) did not match the reported values for the natural product (λmax ≈ 352 and 430 nm). This indicated that the synthesized compound was not identical to the natural product, suggesting a structural misassignment of the natural product.

evidence.text_span: Comparison of the 1H NMR spectroscopic data for synthetic and reported iiso-A2E (8)14 revealed subtle differences, most noticeably at the pyridinium ion region (H15′, H14′, and vicinal hydrogens) and at the methyl groups attached to C(sp)2 (Figure S1 and Tables S1 and S2). Moreover, the UV absorption maxima of synthetic iiso-A2E (8) (λmax ≈ 340 and 430 nm) did not match the reported values for the natural product (λmax ≈ 352 and 430 nm).14

thought.background: The spectroscopic data of synthetic iiso-A2E (8) did not match the natural product, and there was a prior suspicion about the C13'=C14' Z geometry assignment based on 13C NMR chemical shifts.
thought.gap: The correct stereostructure of the natural product needs to be identified.
thought.decision: Hypothesized that the natural product is the C13'=C14' E isomer, named i-A2E (9), and decided to synthesize this alternative stereoisomer.

outcome.observation: Proposed that the natural product is the C13'=C14' E isomer (i-A2E, 9) and planned its synthesis.

evidence.text_span: In view of the lack of correspondence of 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and UV data (Figure S1, Tables S1 and S2, and Figure S2) of synthetic and natural iiso-A2E (8), we considered instead to synthesize the C13′=C14′ E isomer. The proposal took into consideration that the assignment of the double bond geometry of 8 was based on NOE correlations of the C13′-Me substituent (δ ≈ 2.01 ppm) and H14′ substituent (δ ≈ 6.55 ppm) in the 1H NMR spectra. However, the chemical shift values of the signal assigned to C13′ (δ ≈ 12.80 ppm) on the 13C NMR spectra14 seem unlikely to correspond to the Z geometry at the trisubstituted olefin, which usually shows chemical shift values at around δ ≈ 20 ppm,45 and therefore, alternative methyl substituents were likely irradiated in the NOE correlations of the natural sample.

thought.background: To synthesize i-A2E (9), the (E)-alkenylboronate 28 was needed as an intermediate for the Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize (E)-alkenylboronate 28 from protected (E)-3-iodo-2-methylpropenol 26.
thought.decision: Protected (E)-3-iodo-2-methylpropenol 26 was reacted with bis(pinacolato)diboron 27 under Pd(dppf)Cl2·CH2Cl2 catalysis in the presence of KOAc in DMSO at 80 °C.

outcome.observation: (E)-alkenylboronate 28 was formed in 78% yield.

evidence.text_span: Alkenylboronate 28 was previously formed in 78% yield upon heating to 80 °C solutions of protected (E)-3-iodo-2-methylpropenol 2646 in DMSO with bis(pinacolato)diboron 27 under catalysis of Pd(dppf)Cl2·CH2Cl2 in the presence of KOAc.47

thought.background: (E)-alkenylboronate 28 was synthesized. This was to be coupled with 3-bromoisonicotinaldehyde 16 to form (E)-2-alkenylisonicotinaldehyde 29.
thought.gap: Need to perform a Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling reaction.
thought.decision: The Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling of 16 with 28 was promoted by Pd(OAc)2, PPh3, and Na2CO3 in dioxane at 95 °C.

outcome.observation: (E)-2-alkenylisonicotinaldehyde 29 was afforded in 66% yield.

evidence.text_span: The approach to (E)-2-alkenylisonicotinaldehyde 29 differed from the above in the use of the Suzuki−Miyaura cross-coupling reaction40−42 of 16 with (E)-alkenylboronate 28, which was promoted by Pd(OAc)2, PPh3, and Na2CO3 in dioxane at 95 °C (Scheme 3) and afforded 29 in 66% yield.

thought.background: (E)-2-alkenylisonicotinaldehyde 29 was obtained. The next step was to construct the tetraenyl chain using HWE condensation with trienylphosphonate 11.
thought.gap: Need to perform the HWE condensation of 29 with 11.
thought.decision: The HWE condensation of 29 with the conjugated anion of trienylphosphonate 11 was attempted.

outcome.observation: The HWE condensation took place in 89% yield but lacked stereoselectivity, yielding a ca. 1:1 mixture of E/Z double bond isomers (30 and 31, respectively) at the C11=C12 bond.

evidence.text_span: The classical HWE condensation of stereoisomer 29 with the conjugated anion of trienylphosphonate 1118,27,48,49 took place in 89% yield but lacked stereoselectivity in this case, and a ca. 1:1 mixture of E/Z double bond isomers at the C11=C12 bond (30 and 31, respectively) was isolated.

thought.background: The HWE condensation for the tetraenyl chain of the E-isomer (i-A2E) resulted in low stereoselectivity.
thought.gap: A more stereoselective method is needed for the tetraenyl chain construction of the E-isomer.
thought.decision: Switched to a Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling reaction for the construction of the tetraenyl chain, similar to the approach used for the Z-isomer (iiso-A2E).

outcome.observation: Adopted Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling to improve stereoselectivity for the tetraenyl chain of the E-isomer.

evidence.text_span: As described for the Z isomer in Scheme 2, the alkenyl−alkenyl Suzuki−Miyaura cross-coupling reaction40−42 of trienyliodide 2244 with alkenylboronic acid 32, which was generated from 29 in 65% yield, led stereoselectively to 30 using catalysis of Pd(PPh3)4 and Tl2CO3 at ambient temperature (Scheme 3) in 78% yield.

thought.background: To perform the Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling for the E-isomer, alkenylboronic acid 32 was needed, which could be generated from 29.
thought.gap: Need to convert 29 into alkenylboronic acid 32.
thought.decision: Alkenylboronic acid 32 was generated from 29 using bis[(pinacolato)boryl]methane 20, n-BuLi, and TMP in THF at -78 °C.

outcome.observation: Alkenylboronic acid 32 was generated in 65% yield.

evidence.text_span: As described for the Z isomer in Scheme 2, the alkenyl−alkenyl Suzuki−Miyaura cross-coupling reaction40−42 of trienyliodide 2244 with alkenylboronic acid 32, which was generated from 29 in 65% yield, led stereoselectively to 30 using catalysis of Pd(PPh3)4 and Tl2CO3 at ambient temperature (Scheme 3) in 78% yield.

thought.background: Alkenylboronic acid 32 was generated. It needed to be coupled with trienyliodide 22 to form the tetraenylpyridine 30.
thought.gap: Need to perform the Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling reaction.
thought.decision: The Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling of 32 with 22 was performed using Pd(PPh3)4 and Tl2CO3 at ambient temperature.

outcome.observation: Stereoselectively, 30 was obtained in 78% yield.

evidence.text_span: As described for the Z isomer in Scheme 2, the alkenyl−alkenyl Suzuki−Miyaura cross-coupling reaction40−42 of trienyliodide 2244 with alkenylboronic acid 32, which was generated from 29 in 65% yield, led stereoselectively to 30 using catalysis of Pd(PPh3)4 and Tl2CO3 at ambient temperature (Scheme 3) in 78% yield.

thought.background: Intermediate 30 was obtained. The silyl protecting group needed to be removed.
thought.gap: Need to deprotect 30 to form 33.
thought.decision: Deprotection of 30 was carried out using HCO2H in H2O/CH3CN at 0 °C for 15 hours.

outcome.observation: Deprotection of 30 yielded 33 in 80% yield.

evidence.text_span: The construction of the longer pentaenyl branch of the target followed the same steps (deprotection, oxidation, and stereoselective HWE condensation) described for the Z isomer, using the reagents and reaction conditions described above, and afforded bis-polyenylpyridine 35 in higher yields.

thought.background: Allylic alcohol 33 was obtained. It needed to be oxidized to the corresponding enal 34.
thought.gap: Need to perform allylic alcohol oxidation of 33.
thought.decision: Allylic alcohol oxidation of 33 was performed using MnO2 with Na2CO3 in CH2Cl2 at 25 °C for 1 hour.

outcome.observation: Enal 34 was afforded in 72% yield.

evidence.text_span: The construction of the longer pentaenyl branch of the target followed the same steps (deprotection, oxidation, and stereoselective HWE condensation) described for the Z isomer, using the reagents and reaction conditions described above, and afforded bis-polyenylpyridine 35 in higher yields.

thought.background: Enal 34 was obtained. The final polyene chain (pentaene) needed to be constructed using HWE condensation with phosphonate 11.
thought.gap: Need to perform the HWE condensation of 34 with 11.
thought.decision: The HWE condensation of 34 with 11 was performed using NaHMDS (1.60 equiv) and DMPU in THF at -30 to 25 °C for 16 hours.

outcome.observation: Bis-polyenylpyridine 35 was afforded in 96% yield. This reaction was stereoselective.

evidence.text_span: The construction of the longer pentaenyl branch of the target followed the same steps (deprotection, oxidation, and stereoselective HWE condensation) described for the Z isomer, using the reagents and reaction conditions described above, and afforded bis-polyenylpyridine 35 in higher yields.

thought.background: Bis-polyenylpyridine 35 was synthesized. The final step was pyridine alkylation to form the target pyridinium salt 9 (i-A2E).
thought.gap: Need to alkylate pyridine 35 with 2-iodoethanol (25).
thought.decision: Alkylation of 35 with 2-iodoethanol 25 was performed in nitromethane at 60 °C for 12 hours.

outcome.observation: Targeted pyridinium salt 9 (i-A2E) was generated in 72% yield.

evidence.text_span: Alkylation of 35 with 2-iodoethanol 25 in nitromethane at 60 °C for 12 h generated targeted pyridinium salt 9 in 72% yield (Scheme 3).

thought.background: Synthetic i-A2E (9) was obtained.
thought.gap: Need to compare the spectroscopic data of synthetic 9 with the reported data for natural iiso-A2E (8).
thought.decision: 1H NMR and UV spectroscopic data were compared.

outcome.observation: Excellent superimposition was noted upon comparison of the 1H NMR and UV spectroscopic data for synthetic pyridinium bis-retinoid 9 and those reported for iiso-A2E (8). This confirmed that the natural material is indeed the C13'=C14' E isomer, i.e., i-A2E (9), thus correcting the proposed structure.

evidence.text_span: Excellent superimposition was noted upon comparison of the 1H NMR (Figure S1 and Tables S1 and S2) and UV (Figure S2) spectroscopic data for synthetic pyridinium bis-retinoid 9 and those reported for iiso-A2E (8),14 which confirmed that the natural material is indeed the C13′=C14′ E isomer of the proposed structure, namely i-A2E.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The synthetic i-A2E (9) showed excellent superimposition with the reported 1H NMR and UV spectroscopic data for the natural product previously identified as iiso-A2E (8). This led to the stereochemical reassignment of the natural product from iiso-A2E (8) to i-A2E (9). The UV data for i-A2E (9) (λmax ≈ 348 and 430 nm) further confirmed that the longer unsaturated arm exhibits a hypsochromic shift relative to A2E (6) (λmax ≈ 440 nm), with absorption maxima similar to those of Z isomer iso-A2E (7) (λmax ≈ 430 nm).
validation.significance: The total synthesis and stereochemical reassignment of iiso-A2E to i-A2E (9) is crucial for understanding the role of these bisretinoids in ocular diseases like Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD). The corrected structure provides accurate information for future studies on its biogenesis, biological activity, and potential therapeutic interventions. The work also highlights the importance of total synthesis in validating and correcting natural product structures.

source.title: Stereochemical Reassignment by Total Synthesis of an Ocular Pyridinium Bisretinoid of Retinal Pigment Epithelium Lipofuscin: <i>iiso</i>-A2E Is <i>i</i>-A2E.
source.authors: Vidal B, Iglesias-Menduiña O, Vaz B, Álvarez R, Martínez C, de Lera ÁR.
